title: Worked Example
text: This work example demonstrates the step-by-step of creating the appropriate input files for the Small-Area Estimation (SAE) methods to follow. In the process, we will also address the various decisions that researchers need to make.

title: Worked Example
text:
1 Setup

1.1 Understanding the tutorial datasets
The National Survey for Wales 2022-23 is a cross-sectional survey, conducted annually to provide a snapshot of the Welsh population, covering topics include health, education, climate change, visits to the outdoors, public service satisfaction, material deprivation, internet access, and the Welsh language.
The survey dataset used in this tutorial simulates the NSW microdata by treating it with stratified randomisation and randomised upsampling to both ensure data privacy and create a more granular spatial identifier for tutorial purposes, since estimation cannot be performed at a finer spatial scale than what the survey provides.






Note



In the original dataset, the Local Authority (LA) is the sole spatial identifier and the survey’s stratification variable. The example simulated dataset was created using:

Stratified randomisation: Survey responses were first split by Local Authority and their variables were randomised column-wise to preserve totals by Local Authority.
Randomised upsampling: Survey responses were assigned a random MSOA that intersects with their recorded Local Authority to simulate a higher-resolution dataset for tutorial purposes.






1.2 Defining the estimands
The most important first step is to define the estimands, i.e. what is being estimated, for which population, and at what geography. For this tutorial, we specifically want to estimate indicators related to commuting, bus usage, and active travel behaviour in Wales. The estimands could be expressed like this:










Est. #
Measure
Outcome
Target population
Estimation geography




1
Mean
Commute distance
Commuters
MSOAs in Wales


2
Share
Dissatisfaction with bus services
Adults who rode a bus in the last 12 months
MSOAs in Wales


3
Rate
Walking as main transport at least weekly per 1,000 residents
All residents
MSOAs in Wales



Specifying these elements early is critical as they dictate the technical requirements for the estimation process.
For example:

Measure: Estimating a mean or share can be done directly with a compatible analysis weight. Estimating a population rate with a known denominator requires a grossing weight, so the weighted total can be divided by the known domain size.
Weight: Estimating the share of recent adult bus riders dissatisfied with bus services requires a weight calibrated for the adult population, since applying a general population weight would bias the result by including children.
Geography: With a sample size of 11,000 respondents in Wales, if we choose to estimate at the OA level (~10,000 small areas), we could leave the vast majority of OAs with zero or one respondent.







Tip



In practice, this step is reiterative based on data availability and other restrictions. It is worth starting out with the most relevant estimands and refining them further after diagnosing the quality of the estimation results.




1.3 Preparing indicators and weight variables
The next stage involves preparing the indicator variables and aligning them with the appropriate estimation geography and survey weights, as defined by the estimand.











Est. #
Indicator
Variable coding rule
Measure
Estimation geography
Weight variable




1
commute_distance
[TravWkDist] Numeric commute distance in miles, non-commuters are coded NA
Mean
MSOA
SampleAdultWeight, analysis weight


2
poor_bus_satisfaction
[BusOverSat, Bus12M] Binary: 1 if rode a bus in the last 12 months and is fairly or very dissatisfied; 0 if rode a bus in the last 12 months and is not dissatisfied; NA otherwise
Share
MSOA
SampleAdultWeight, analysis weight


3
walk_weekly
[AtFrqWlk10] Binary: 1 if walking for transport at least weekly
Rate (per 1k)
MSOA
SampleTravelWeight, analysis weight; to be scaled to grossing weight



As a reminder, analysis weights support weighted means and proportions. A grossing weight scales survey respondents up to a known population total. In this dataset, weight variables are chosen for each indicator based on definitional compatibility:

SampleAdultWeight: an analysis weight that adjusts for sample design bias to reflect real-world means and proportions. It is used here for commute_distance and poor_bus_satisfaction.
SampleTravelWeight: an analysis weight for the active-travel module. It is exported for walk_weekly, then scaled to a Wales-wide grossing weight in the direct-estimation workbook.



1.4 Selecting covariates
Covariates, or auxiliary variables, are the backbone of SAE because they lend information to areas with low or no survey data to improve their precision. Covariates should be available for every estimation areas and should have a clear relationship to the estimands.
The Census 2021 bulk downloads are a practical starting point because they provide rich and consistent datasets aggregated at all UK geographies.
For this worked example, each indicator will have at its disposal covariates that are its most logical predictors, as follows:









Est. #
Indicator
Covariates (from UK Census ’21)





1
commute_distance

- Population density (TS006)- Share of commute by car/van (TS061)- Share of households with car/van (TS045)


2
poor_bus_satisfaction
- Population density (TS006)- Share of commute by bus/minibus (TS061)- Share of households without car/van (TS045)- Share of households deprived in 2+ dimensions (TS011)
MSOA


3
walk_weekly
- Population density (TS006)- Share of commute by bike/walk (TS061)- Share of households without car/van (TS045)- Share of population in good health (TS037)
MSOA



With this information we can proceed to process the survey dataset to produce inputs necessary for the workflow.

title: Worked Example
text:
2 Processing

library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(stringr)
library(ggplot2)

# Download raw input files into a temp folder
zip_tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".zip")
url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/shaunhoang/tutorial-site/main/data/tutorial-raw-data.zip"
download.file(url, zip_tmp, mode = "wb")
unzip(zip_tmp, exdir = tempdir())
base_path <- file.path(tempdir(), "raw")


2.1 Load survey data
Load the survey datasets and identify the unique identifier of survey respondents, and recode it as unit_id for easier handling downstream.

nsw_raw <- read_csv(
  file.path(base_path, "nsw_2223_msoa_la.csv")
) |>
  rename(unit_id = CaseNo)

For each respondent, the survey provides two spatial identifiers: Local Authority and MSOA. The three estimands are estimated at MSOA level, while Local Authority remains useful as the survey stratification variable.
The survey is stratified by Local Authority, so la_domain_id is also used as the strata column in the exported indicator files. If a survey has no stratification variable, set strata = "all" instead.

survey_spatial_ids <- nsw_raw |>
  transmute(
    unit_id,
    la_domain_id = la_code,
    msoa_domain_id = msoa_code
  )

head(survey_spatial_ids)

# A tibble: 6 × 3
    unit_id la_domain_id msoa_domain_id
      <dbl> <chr>        <chr>         
1 222300001 W06000001    W02000005     
2 222300002 W06000001    W02000002     
3 222300003 W06000001    W02000002     
4 222300004 W06000001    W02000007     
5 222300005 W06000001    W02000005     
6 222300006 W06000001    W02000009     




2.2 Load MSOA population totals
Next, load the Census population totals for Welsh MSOAs.
In this exercise, walk_weekly uses the MSOA population as the known denominator. The same file also gives us the full list of target MSOAs and provides a useful population covariate.

Total population by MSOA


domain_sizes_population_msoa <- read_csv(
  file.path(base_path, "census2021-ts007-msoa.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  filter(str_starts(`geography code`, "W")) |>
  transmute(
    domain_id = `geography code`,
    domain_name = geography,
    domain_size = `Age: Total; measures: Value`
  )
  
head(domain_sizes_population_msoa)

# A tibble: 6 × 3
  domain_id domain_name          domain_size
  <chr>     <chr>                      <dbl>
1 W02000001 Isle of Anglesey 001        8402
2 W02000002 Isle of Anglesey 002        6367
3 W02000003 Isle of Anglesey 003       10633
4 W02000004 Isle of Anglesey 004        7401
5 W02000005 Isle of Anglesey 005        8165
6 W02000006 Isle of Anglesey 006        7042




2.3 Construct Indicators
Next, we can start constructing the indicators from the variables based on the coding rules set out above.

# Define a helper to handle the NSW specific missing codes
clean_nsw <- function(x) {
  ifelse(x %in% c(-99, -98, -9, -8, -7), NA, x)
}

survey_indicators <- nsw_raw |>
  mutate(
    commute_distance_raw = clean_nsw(TravWkDist),
    bus_used_12m = clean_nsw(Bus12M),
    bus_satisfaction = clean_nsw(BusOverSat),
    walk_frequency = clean_nsw(AtFrqWlk10)
  ) |>
  transmute(
    unit_id,
    commute_distance = as.numeric(commute_distance_raw),

    poor_bus_satisfaction = case_when(
      bus_used_12m != 1 ~ NA_real_,
      bus_satisfaction %in% c(4, 5) ~ 1,
      !is.na(bus_satisfaction) ~ 0,
      TRUE ~ NA_real_
    ),

    walk_weekly = case_when(
      is.na(walk_frequency) ~ NA_real_,
      walk_frequency %in% 1:3 ~ 1,
      TRUE ~ 0
    )
  )


head(survey_indicators)

# A tibble: 6 × 4
    unit_id commute_distance poor_bus_satisfaction walk_weekly
      <dbl>            <dbl>                 <dbl>       <dbl>
1 222300001               NA                    NA          NA
2 222300002               NA                    NA          NA
3 222300003               NA                    NA          NA
4 222300004               NA                    NA          NA
5 222300005               NA                     1          NA
6 222300006               NA                    NA           1




2.4 Select weight variables
Next, filter for the relevant weight variables from the survey relevant to our estimands.

survey_weights <- nsw_raw |>
  transmute(
    unit_id,
    SampleAdultWeight,
    SampleTravelWeight
  )

SampleTravelWeight is an analysis weight for the active-travel module (subsampled). The survey user guide describes it as adjusting the active-travel sample to have the same age/sex distribution as the general population. We will need a grossing weight to scale the survey data to the total population in order to calculate rate per 1k.


2.5 Check missingness
For each indicator, check for missingness overall (number of null values) and whether there are areas with no respondents.

# Join the coded indicators and calculated weights together
survey_inputs <- survey_spatial_ids |>
  left_join(survey_indicators, by = "unit_id") |>
  left_join(survey_weights, by = "unit_id")

# Calculate non-nulls and coverage for each indicator at estimation geography
indicator_observations <- bind_rows(
  survey_inputs |>
    transmute(
      indicator = "commute_distance",
      domain_id = msoa_domain_id,
      value = commute_distance,
      weight = SampleAdultWeight
    ),
  survey_inputs |>
    transmute(
      indicator = "poor_bus_satisfaction",
      domain_id = msoa_domain_id,
      value = poor_bus_satisfaction,
      weight = SampleAdultWeight
    ),
  survey_inputs |>
    transmute(
      indicator = "walk_weekly",
      domain_id = msoa_domain_id,
      value = walk_weekly,
      weight = SampleTravelWeight
    )
)

indicator_area_summary <- indicator_observations |>
  filter(!is.na(value), !is.na(weight)) |>
  count(indicator, domain_id, name = "non_missing") |>
  group_by(indicator) |>
  summarise(
    geography = "MSOA",
    areas_total = nrow(domain_sizes_population_msoa),
    areas_no_data = areas_total - n_distinct(domain_id),
    areas_small_samp = areas_no_data + sum(non_missing <= 2),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

indicator_area_summary

# A tibble: 3 × 5
  indicator             geography areas_total areas_no_data areas_small_samp
  <chr>                 <chr>           <int>         <int>            <int>
1 commute_distance      MSOA              408             1                9
2 poor_bus_satisfaction MSOA              408             1                5
3 walk_weekly           MSOA              408             5               76


This summary highlights areas with no or few data (n <= 2). For two indicators, poor_bus_satisfaction and commute_distance, the number of small-sample areas is below 10, which is not a major concern at this stage. walk_weekly has many more small-sample areas.

indicator_area_counts <- indicator_observations |>
  filter(!is.na(value), !is.na(weight)) |>
  count(indicator, domain_id, name = "non_missing")

ggplot(indicator_area_counts, aes(x = non_missing)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, boundary = 0, fill = "#4C78A8", colour = "white") +
  facet_wrap(~ indicator, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    x = "Usable observations per MSOA",
    y = "Number of MSOAs"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()




This is consistent with the fact that the active-travel questions used to construct walk_weekly were only asked to a subsample. It will probably benefit the most from model-based estimation later, although the final decision is best made after looking at the direct estimates and their uncertainty.


2.6 Aggregate covariates
The final preparation step is to assemble the covariates chosen previously for each estimand and aggregate them to the estimation geography required (in our case, MSOA).
Because all three indicators are estimated at MSOA level, their covariates can be combined into one.

# Read a Census 2021 aggregate table and keep Welsh areas only
read_census <- function(topic_code, geo_level) {
  file_name <- paste0("census2021-", topic_code, "-", geo_level, ".csv")
  
  read_csv(file.path(base_path, file_name), show_col_types = FALSE) |>
    filter(str_starts(`geography code`, "W")) |>
    rename(domain_id = `geography code`, domain_name = geography)
}


# Total population (TS007)
msoa_population <- domain_sizes_population_msoa |>
  transmute(
    domain_id,
    cov_total_population = domain_size
  )

# Population density (TS006)
msoa_density <- read_census("ts006", "msoa") |>
  transmute(
    domain_id,
    domain_name,
    cov_pop_density = `Population Density: Persons per square kilometre; measures: Value`
  )

# Travel to work (TS061)
msoa_travel <- read_census("ts061", "msoa") |>
  transmute(
    domain_id,
    commute_total = `Method of travel to workplace: Total: All usual residents aged 16 years and over in employment the week before the census`,
    commute_car = `Method of travel to workplace: Driving a car or van` +
      `Method of travel to workplace: Passenger in a car or van`,
    commute_bus = `Method of travel to workplace: Bus, minibus or coach`,
    commute_bikewalk = `Method of travel to workplace: Bicycle` +
      `Method of travel to workplace: On foot`,
    cov_share_car_commute = if_else(commute_total > 0, commute_car / commute_total, NA_real_),
    cov_share_bus_commute = if_else(commute_total > 0, commute_bus / commute_total, NA_real_),
    cov_share_commute_bikewalk = if_else(commute_total > 0, commute_bikewalk / commute_total, NA_real_)
  ) |>
  select(
    domain_id,
    cov_share_car_commute,
    cov_share_bus_commute,
    cov_share_commute_bikewalk
  )

# Car availability (TS045)
msoa_cars <- read_census("ts045", "msoa") |>
  transmute(
    domain_id,
    household_total = `Number of cars or vans: Total: All households`,
    households_no_car = `Number of cars or vans: No cars or vans in household`,
    cov_share_hh_nocar = if_else(
      household_total > 0,
      households_no_car / household_total,
      NA_real_
    ),
    cov_share_hh_withcar = if_else(
      household_total > 0,
      (household_total - households_no_car) / household_total,
      NA_real_
    )
  ) |>
  select(
    domain_id,
    cov_share_hh_nocar,
    cov_share_hh_withcar
  )

# Household deprivation (TS011)
msoa_deprivation <- read_census("ts011", "msoa") |>
  transmute(
    domain_id,
    household_total = `Household deprivation: Total: All households; measures: Value`,
    households_deprived = `Household deprivation: Household is deprived in two dimensions; measures: Value` +
      `Household deprivation: Household is deprived in three dimensions; measures: Value` +
      `Household deprivation: Household is deprived in four dimensions; measures: Value`,
    cov_share_hh_deprived = if_else(
      household_total > 0,
      households_deprived / household_total,
      NA_real_
    )
  ) |>
  select(domain_id, cov_share_hh_deprived)

# Health (TS037)
msoa_health <- read_census("ts037", "msoa") |>
  transmute(
    domain_id,
    health_total = `General health: Total: All usual residents`,
    good_health = `General health: Very good health` + `General health: Good health`,
    cov_share_good_health = if_else(
      health_total > 0,
      good_health / health_total,
      NA_real_
    )
  ) |>
  select(domain_id, cov_share_good_health)

covariates_msoa <- list(
  msoa_density,
  msoa_population,
  msoa_travel,
  msoa_cars,
  msoa_deprivation,
  msoa_health
) |>
  purrr::reduce(left_join, by = "domain_id")

covariates_msoa

# A tibble: 408 × 11
   domain_id domain_name          cov_pop_density cov_total_population
   <chr>     <chr>                          <dbl>                <dbl>
 1 W02000001 Isle of Anglesey 001            62.8                 8402
 2 W02000002 Isle of Anglesey 002            53.1                 6367
 3 W02000003 Isle of Anglesey 003          2298.                 10633
 4 W02000004 Isle of Anglesey 004            99.2                 7401
 5 W02000005 Isle of Anglesey 005           100                   8165
 6 W02000006 Isle of Anglesey 006           152.                  7042
 7 W02000007 Isle of Anglesey 007            68.5                 6894
 8 W02000008 Isle of Anglesey 008           831.                  5951
 9 W02000009 Isle of Anglesey 009            55.9                 8035
10 W02000010 Gwynedd 001                   3112.                  8626
# ℹ 398 more rows
# ℹ 7 more variables: cov_share_car_commute <dbl>, cov_share_bus_commute <dbl>,
#   cov_share_commute_bikewalk <dbl>, cov_share_hh_nocar <dbl>,
#   cov_share_hh_withcar <dbl>, cov_share_hh_deprived <dbl>,
#   cov_share_good_health <dbl>




2.7 Export
The next workbooks do not depend on local exports from this workbook. However, if preferred, export into your data/clean/ folder the following components:
Indicator microdata with weight and survey strata

ind_weight_commute_distance.csv, with analysis adult weight;
ind_weight_poor_bus_satisfaction.csv, with analysis adult weight;
ind_weight_walk_weekly.csv, with active-travel module analysis weight, which will be scaled to a Wales-wide grossing weight during direct estimation.

Each indicator microdata file contains the same key columns:







Column
Meaning




unit_id
Survey respondent identifier.


domain_id
Estimation area ID.


strata
Survey stratification variable. In this tutorial, this is la_domain_id.


indicator
Coded outcome variable.


weight
Survey weight matched to the estimand.



Covariates by estimation area

covariates_msoa.csv, with MSOA-level covariates for all three indicators;

Domain list, denominator, and covariate input

domain_sizes_population_msoa.csv, for the full list of target MSOAs, the walk_weekly denominator, and the total-population covariate.


# eval: false
output_dir <- file.path("..","data", "clean")
dir.create(output_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

ind_weight_commute_distance <- survey_inputs |>
  transmute(
    unit_id,
    domain_id = msoa_domain_id,
    strata = la_domain_id,
    indicator = commute_distance,
    weight = SampleAdultWeight
  )

ind_weight_poor_bus_satisfaction <- survey_inputs |>
  transmute(
    unit_id,
    domain_id = msoa_domain_id,
    strata = la_domain_id,
    indicator = poor_bus_satisfaction,
    weight = SampleAdultWeight
  )

ind_weight_walk_weekly <- survey_inputs |>
  transmute(
    unit_id,
    domain_id = msoa_domain_id,
    strata = la_domain_id,
    indicator = walk_weekly,
    weight = SampleTravelWeight
  )

write_csv(
  ind_weight_commute_distance,
  file.path(output_dir, "ind_weight_commute_distance.csv")
)

write_csv(
  ind_weight_poor_bus_satisfaction,
  file.path(output_dir, "ind_weight_poor_bus_satisfaction.csv")
)

write_csv(
  ind_weight_walk_weekly,
  file.path(output_dir, "ind_weight_walk_weekly.csv")
)

write_csv(
  domain_sizes_population_msoa |>
    select(domain_id, domain_size) |>
    as.data.frame(),
  file.path(output_dir, "domain_sizes_population_msoa.csv")
)

write_csv(
  as.data.frame(covariates_msoa),
  file.path(output_dir, "covariates_msoa.csv")
)

title: Unit-Level Estimation
text: Unit-level SAE models use survey microdata directly rather than starting from area-level direct estimates. They can be powerful, but they require much stronger data conditions than area-level Fay-Herriot models.

title: Unit-Level Estimation
text:
1 Understanding Unit-Level Estimation
In area-level Small Area Estimation, the model starts from one direct estimate per estimation area. In unit-level Small Area Estimation, the model starts from individual or household survey records:
\[
y_{di} =
x_{di}^{\top}\beta + u_d + e_{di},
\]
where \(y_{di}\) is the outcome for unit \(i\) in estimation area \(d\), \(x_{di}\) is a vector of unit-level covariates, \(u_d\) is the area effect, and \(e_{di}\) is unit-level noise. For binary outcomes, the model is usually written as a logistic or probit mixed model.
The area estimate is then built by predicting or simulating values for the population units in each estimation area and aggregating them:
\[
\theta_d =
\frac{1}{N_d}
\sum_{i \in U_d} y_{di}.
\]
This makes unit-level SAE a bottom-up approach: model people or households first, predict for the whole target population, and then aggregate to the chosen estimation geography.
[ADD IMAGE]

title: Unit-Level Estimation
text:
2 Caveats with Unit-Level Estimation
Unit-level SAE is useful when the survey and population data share comparable individual, household, and covariates. It is most appropriate when the outcome is strongly related to person- or household-level characteristics rather than to an area, or when estimates may need to be aggregated to more than one geography.






Pros:

Uses individual or household covariates directly, rather than area averages
Can aggregate to several geographic scales

Cons:

Survey and population datasets need to be harmonised and include the same covariates
More sensitive estimates because predictions are made for many non-sampled units and scaled up to population level
More technically demanding, especially in terms of privacy/access




In general, prefer Area-level SAE when only area-level covariates are available. Unit-level SAE is more realistic when suitable population microdata, or a validated synthetic population can provide the prediction population.

title: Unit-Level Estimation
text:
3 Code Implementation






Tip



Use SUMMER::smoothUnit() to fit a basic unit-level SAE model from a survey::svydesign() object and population unit-level covariates. It supports using an adjacency matrix for spatial structure.

title: Overview
text:
Raw survey microdata is transformed into analysis-ready inputs by defining the estimand, coding the indicator from the survey variables, aligning it with the estimation geography, and choosing a compatible survey weight.
The data preparation pipeline can be summarised as follows:

title: Overview
text:
1 Define the Estimands
Before fitting any SAE model, define exactly what we are estimating. This estimand determines how survey microdata inputs are prepared and how to choose the most appropriate estimation approach downstream.
It is good practice to formulate the estimands of interest in this format:

Estimate the measure of what outcome, among which population, across which estimation geography.

E.g.,

Share of adults who use the train weekly by MSOA
Mean leisure travel distance among retirees by Ward


1.1 Measure
Estimands can take on any of these types of measures which determine how the outcome will be aggregated at the desired estimation geography.

share
mean
count
rate (e.g., trips per adults)
ratio (e.g., accidents per 1,000 residents)



1.2 Outcome
Based on the estimands, identify or create the survey indicator that measures the outcome. Here are some of the most common indicator types:








Indicator type
What to define
Example




Binary
What is 1, 0, missing, and out of scope. Also suitable for categorical variables
Estimand: Share of adults who use the train weeklyIndicator: Uses train weekly: yes/no


Continuous
The numeric value and unit
Estimand: Mean leisure travel distance among retirees Indicator: Leisure travel distance in miles



Use the survey questionnaire, response codes, and routing rules to guide indicator construction. Some indicators are direct recodes of one survey question; others need to combine several variables. The same raw survey variable can support different estimands.


1.3 Estimation geography
In SAE, the groups or areas being estimated are often called domains. In the spatial context such as urban and transport research, they are better understood as estimation areas instead. An estimation area is one unit in the chosen estimation geography, such as one Local Authority, Ward, LSOA, or MSOA.



Census Geography hierarchy going from Country to LA to MSOA to LSOA to OA (Source)


The estimation geography must also match the spatial detail available in the survey. For example, if respondents are only identified by Local Authority, then Local Authority is the finest geography available for SAE.

title: Overview
text:
2 Weights and domain sizes
Survey weights connect the sample back to the population to ensure the survey sample can best represent real-world proportions.
In general, weight variables provided by surveys have already been derived from one or more adjustments, such as:

Selection adjustment: accounts for unequal probabilities of selection.
Non-response adjustment: compensates for differences between respondents and non-respondents.
Calibration adjustment: aligns the weighted sample with known population totals, such as age, sex, region, Local Authority, tenure, or household structure.
Subsample adjustment: accounts for cases where only part of the sample was asked a module or completed a diary.

The survey user guide is the go-to resource to understand which weight variables are available, what they adjust for, and most importantly, which survey modules/questions they apply to.
For SAE, there is no one-size-fits-all approach to survey weight selection. The weight used must match the estimand unit, target population, and survey design.
Moreover, it is important to distinguish the scale of the weight variable:

Analysis weights \(w^{A}\) make means or proportions representative of the target population, and they generally sum to the sample size. This type of weight variables are most common for estimating share or mean.

E.g., if a sampling unit has an analysis weight of 0.5, it is over-represented in the sample relative to the population, so their contribution is ‘down-weighted’.

Grossing weights \(w^{G}\) are essentially scaled-\(w^{A}\) so that they sum to a known population total within the relevant estimation area. They are required if the estimand measure is count, rate (e.g., x per 1,000 adults).

E.g., if a sampling unit has a grossing weight of 50, it means this one sampled row represents about 50 people in the population.


To calculate count, rate estimands, we not only need grossing weights to scale the sample to population totals, we also need to know these population totals at the chosen estimation geography.
In SAE, these totals are called domain sizes \(N_d\), i.e.,the size of the target population in each estimation area, usually obtained from Census data or other regional statistics.
E.g.,

All residents by MSOA
All adults (16+) by LA
All commuters by Region







Tip



If analysis weights are provided and the domain sizes \(N_d\) are known, analysis weights can be scaled to grossing weights for each survey respondent using the following formula: \[
w^G_{di} = w^A_{di} * \frac{N_d}{\sum w^A_d}
\]

title: Overview
text:
3 Select covariates
SAE improves precision by “borrowing strength” from auxiliary variables (i.e., covariates). This is critical for estimation areas with sparse or non-existent survey data.
Area-level covariates can often be found via census data and other population-level aggregate statistics. Choose covariates with emphasis on coverage and comparability across estimation areas.

Coverage: each covariate should be available for all estimation areas.
Alignment: match geography, definition, and reference period to the survey indicator.
Relevance: keep variables with a clear substantive link to the outcome and avoid including many weak predictors.
Predictive: choose covariates that improve area-level prediction and are easy to explain.

For example, some common covariates when estimating transport-related indicators are: age, income, employment profile, population density, housing mix, deprivation, urban/rural classification, transport accessibility metrics, car ownership, etc.

title: Overview
text:
4 Generate analysis-ready inputs
Putting everything together, each of the estimands of interest should have a short record as follows that will guide how we prepare input datasets for the SAE workflow. For example, a compact record could be written as:













#
Estimand
Indicator
Variable coding rule
Measure
Estimation geography
Weight variable
Covariates




1
Share of adults who use the train weekly across MSOAs
is_freq_train_user
Binary: 1 if TrainUseFreq = 1; 0 if adult does not use the train weekly; null for invalid or refused responses
Share
MSOA
SampleAdultWeight
age,income,own_carvan, etc.


2
Mean leisure travel distance among retirees across Wards
leisure_travel_distance
Continuous: numeric miles from TravelWorkDistance; only retirees with valid leisure travel distance are in scope
Mean
Ward
LeisureTravelModuleWeight
income,own_home,own_carvan, etc.



The next workbook will demonstrate a worked example using a simulated National Survey for Wales 2022-2023 respondent microdata.

title: [WIP] FAQs
text: Lorem Ipsum

title: Direct Estimation
text: Direct estimation is the process of deriving a preliminary estimate at the desired estimation geography, based solely on the survey data. The direct estimates and their variances are also components in model-based area-level estimation in the next workbook.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
1 Direct Estimator and Sampling Variances
The estimator should match the information available in the survey file and the estimand definition.









Scenario
Estimator
Formula
When to use




Known domain sizes; has grossing weights
Horvitz-Thompson estimator
\(\hat{\theta}_d^{DIR}=\frac{\sum_{i\in s_d}w^G_{di}y_{di}}{N_d}\)
Anchors estimates to the true population size of each estimation area; required for counts/totals and useful for population rates, but equally valid for means or proportions


Unknown domain sizes; has survey weights
Hájek estimator
\(\hat{\theta}_d^{DIR}=\frac{\sum_{i \in s_d}w_{di}^Ay_{di}}{\sum_{i \in s_d}w^A_{di}}\)
Can only be used for estimating means or proportions when only using analysis weights and no reliable area population size is available


Unknown domain sizes; no survey weights
Unweight sample mean
\(\hat{\theta}_d^{DIR}=\frac{\sum_{i \in s_d}y_{di}}{n_d}\)
The simplest form of direct estimator, likely biased if survey sampling is not random, and imprecise when \(n_d\) is too low



Here, the direct estimate \(\hat{\theta}_d^{DIR}\) is for area \(d\) with sample size \(n_d\) and domain size \(N_d\) if known. Direct estimates are paired with estimated sampling variances, telling us how much uncertainty comes from observing only a sample within each estimation area. The variance depends not only on the estimator, but also the weight scale, and survey design information available.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
2 Evaluate direct estimates
Use the direct estimates and sampling variances to decide whether direct reporting is enough, or whether model-based SAE is needed.



Scenario
Next step




Too many areas have no/few samples, variances are missing or unreasonably large
Revisit the estimand definition (target population, estimation geography)


All areas have direct estimates with acceptable sample sizes, and uncertainty
Direct estimates may be enough for reporting.


Most areas have direct estimates, albeit imprecise. Spatial effects matter.
Fit a Area-Level Estimation Model model to borrow strength from covariates and neighbouring areas.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
3 Code Implementation






Tip



Use survey::svyby() for flexible calculation of direct estimates using any estimator and input survey design elements such as strata.



3.1 Setup

# Load libraries
library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(stringr)
library(tidyr)
library(sf)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)

# Load analysis-ready data into a temp folder
zip_tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".zip")
url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/shaunhoang/tutorial-site/main/data/tutorial-clean-data.zip"
download.file(url, zip_tmp, mode = "wb")
unzip(zip_tmp, exdir = tempdir())
base_path <- file.path(tempdir(), "clean")

# Load shapefiles for plotting
boundary_zip <- tempfile(fileext = ".zip")
boundary_url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/shaunhoang/tutorial-site/main/data/tutorial-boundaries.zip"
download.file(boundary_url, boundary_zip, mode = "wb")
unzip(boundary_zip, exdir = tempdir())

msoa_boundaries <- st_read(
  file.path(tempdir(), "boundaries", "boundaries-msoa.geojson"),
  quiet = TRUE
) |>
  rename(domain_id = MSOA21CD) |>
  filter(str_starts(domain_id, "W"))

msoa_totalpop_domsizes <- read_csv(
  file.path(base_path, "domain_sizes_population_msoa.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  mutate(domain_size = as.numeric(domain_size)) |>
  as.data.frame()

We will first focus on producing and evaluating the estimates using the indicator walk_weekly before replicating the process for poor_bus_satisfaction and commute_distance.


3.2 Estimand: Frequent walkers per 1,000 residents by MSOA

3.2.1 Direct Estimation
Keep in mind that with walk-weekly, we are estimating:

Rate per 1,000 residents walking as main transport mode at least weekly across MSOAs

walk_weekly is a binary survey indicator for whether a respondent walks for transport at least weekly. We first scale SampleTravelWeight to a Wales-wide grossing weight using the total population across Welsh MSOAs. Then we estimate the weighted total number of weekly walkers in each MSOA, divide by the known MSOA population, and multiply by 1,000.

# Load the active-travel indicator with its module analysis weight
survey_indicator_walk <- read_csv(
  file.path(base_path, "ind_weight_walk_weekly.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

# Scale SampleTravelWeight once to the Wales population total
wales_population_total <- sum(msoa_totalpop_domsizes$domain_size, na.rm = TRUE)
travel_weight_total <- sum(survey_indicator_walk$weight, na.rm = TRUE)
wales_travel_scale <- wales_population_total / travel_weight_total

survey_indicator_walk <- survey_indicator_walk |>
  mutate(WalesTravelWeight = weight * wales_travel_scale)

# Check that the scaled weight sums to the target population
survey_indicator_walk |>
  summarise(
    WalesTravelWeight_sum = sum(WalesTravelWeight, na.rm = TRUE),
    Wales_population_total = wales_population_total
  )

# A tibble: 1 × 2
  WalesTravelWeight_sum Wales_population_total
                  <dbl>                  <dbl>
1               3107408                3107408



# Attach the domain size (total pop per MSOA)
survey_indicator_walk <- survey_indicator_walk |>
  left_join(
    msoa_totalpop_domsizes |> select(domain_id, domain_size),
    by = "domain_id"
  )

survey_indicator_walk <- survey_indicator_walk |>
  filter(
    !is.na(indicator),
    !is.na(WalesTravelWeight),
    !is.na(domain_id),
    !is.na(strata),
    !is.na(domain_size)
  )

design_walk <- survey::svydesign(
  ids = ~1,
  strata = ~strata,
  weights = ~WalesTravelWeight,
  data = survey_indicator_walk
)

# Estimate the total number of weekly walkers in each MSOA
walk_total <- survey::svyby(
  formula = ~indicator,
  by = ~domain_id + domain_size,
  design = design_walk,
  FUN = survey::svytotal,
  vartype = "se",
  na.rm = TRUE
) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  tibble::as_tibble()

# Convert the total into a rate per 1,000 residents
dir_est_walk <- walk_total |>
  transmute(
    domain = domain_id,
    direct = (indicator / domain_size) * 1000,
    variance = ((se / domain_size) * 1000)^2,
    cv = if_else(direct == 0, NA_real_, abs(sqrt(variance) / direct)),
    ci_width = 2 * 1.96 * sqrt(variance)
  )

head(dir_est_walk)

# A tibble: 6 × 5
  domain    direct variance    cv ci_width
  <chr>      <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>
1 W02000185   869.  260674. 0.587    2001.
2 W02000306   578.  165372. 0.703    1594.
3 W02000058   929.  283365. 0.573    2087.
4 W02000200  1872. 2723194. 0.881    6469.
5 W02000105   888.   92405. 0.342    1192.
6 W02000056   257.   65861. 1.000    1006.


Alongside the direct estimate (direct) and its sampling variance (variance), the output above also produces

cv for the coefficient of variation calculated as the standard error divided by the estimate. Values >1 indicate that the typical SE is larger than the estimate.
ci_width for the approximate width of the 95% confidence interval (expressed in the original units).



3.2.2 Evaluation
Direct estimation itself is prone to be insufficient. For walk_weekly, the direct estimate depends on only the respondents observed in each MSOA. Small samples can therefore create unstable rates and wide confidence intervals, even though the calculation uses the known MSOA population as the denominator. Since the weight is scaled once for Wales rather than calibrated within each MSOA, some small-sample area estimates may even exceed 1,000 and should be treated as unstable direct estimates.
To assess the quality of the direct estimates, first check whether any estimation areas lack a direct estimate. In some cases, they might have a direct estimate but no useful variance, especially when n = 1. Next, assess whether the available direct estimates are precise enough to report by looking at median_cv and median_ci_width.

walk_map_data <- msoa_boundaries |>
  left_join(
    dir_est_walk,
    by = c("domain_id" = "domain")
  )

walk_estimate_map <- ggplot(walk_map_data) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = direct), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.25) +
  scale_fill_distiller(palette = "YlGnBu", direction = 1, na.value = "grey85") +
  labs(
    fill = "Estimate",
    title = "Direct estimate"
  ) +
  theme_void()

walk_cv_map <- ggplot(walk_map_data) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = cv), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.25) +
  scale_fill_distiller(palette = "OrRd", direction = 1, na.value = "grey85") +
  labs(
    fill = "CV",
    title = "Coefficient of Variation"
  ) +
  theme_void()

walk_estimate_map + walk_cv_map +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Residents walking weekly as main transport mode per 1,000 residents by MSOA"
  )





walk_diagnostics <- msoa_totalpop_domsizes |>
  select(domain_id) |>
  left_join(
    survey_indicator_walk |>
      count(domain_id, name = "n"),
    by = "domain_id"
  ) |>
  left_join(
    dir_est_walk,
    by = c("domain_id" = "domain")
  ) |>
  mutate(n = replace_na(n, 0L))

walk_diagnostics |>
  summarise(
    areas_total = nrow(msoa_totalpop_domsizes),
    areas_no_data = sum(n == 0, na.rm = TRUE),
    areas_n_1 = sum(n == 1, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_sample_size = median(n, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_cv = median(cv, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_ci_width = median(ci_width, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = everything(),
    names_to = "diagnostic",
    values_to = "value"
  )

# A tibble: 6 × 2
  diagnostic            value
  <chr>                 <dbl>
1 areas_total         408    
2 areas_no_data         5    
3 areas_n_1            25    
4 median_sample_size    5    
5 median_cv             0.708
6 median_ci_width    1165.   





3.3 Estimand: Mean commute distance among commuters by MSOA

3.3.1 Direct Estimation
The indicator commute_distance is continuous, so the direct estimate is the weighted mean commute distance among available commuter respondents.

survey_indicator <- read_csv(
  file.path(base_path, "ind_weight_commute_distance.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  filter(!is.na(indicator), !is.na(weight), !is.na(domain_id), !is.na(strata))

design_commute <- survey::svydesign(
  ids = ~1,
  strata = ~strata,
  weights = ~weight,
  data = survey_indicator
)

commute_mean <- survey::svyby(
  formula = ~indicator,
  by = ~domain_id,
  design = design_commute,
  FUN = survey::svymean,
  vartype = "se",
  na.rm = TRUE
) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  tibble::as_tibble()

dir_est_commute <- commute_mean |>
  transmute(
    domain = domain_id,
    direct = indicator,
    variance = se^2,
    cv = if_else(direct == 0, NA_real_, abs(sqrt(variance) / direct)),
    ci_width = 2 * 1.96 * sqrt(variance)
  )

head(dir_est_commute)

# A tibble: 6 × 5
  domain    direct variance    cv ci_width
  <chr>      <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>
1 W02000001   9.70     6.77 0.268    10.2 
2 W02000002  11.2      1.84 0.121     5.31
3 W02000003  10.6      7.56 0.258    10.8 
4 W02000005  25.3    101.   0.396    39.4 
5 W02000006  35.1    528.   0.654    90.1 
6 W02000007   9.91     4.70 0.219     8.49




3.3.2 Evaluation







# A tibble: 6 × 2
  diagnostic           value
  <chr>                <dbl>
1 areas_total        408    
2 areas_no_data        1    
3 areas_n_1            1    
4 median_sample_size   8.5  
5 median_cv            0.318
6 median_ci_width     11.9  





3.4 Estimand: Dissatisfaction with bus service among recent adult bus riders by MSOA
The indicator poor_bus_satisfaction is binary. It is defined only among adults who rode a bus in the last 12 months: 1 means dissatisfied, 0 means not dissatisfied, and non-riders are left as missing. The direct estimate is the weighted proportion among available recent bus riders.

3.4.1 Direct Estimation

survey_indicator_bus <- read_csv(
  file.path(base_path, "ind_weight_poor_bus_satisfaction.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  filter(!is.na(indicator), !is.na(weight), !is.na(domain_id), !is.na(strata))

design_bus <- survey::svydesign(
  ids = ~1,
  strata = ~strata,
  weights = ~weight,
  data = survey_indicator_bus
)

bus_mean <- survey::svyby(
  formula = ~indicator,
  by = ~domain_id,
  design = design_bus,
  FUN = survey::svymean,
  vartype = "se",
  na.rm = TRUE
) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  tibble::as_tibble()

dir_est_bus <- bus_mean |>
  transmute(
    domain = domain_id,
    direct = indicator,
    variance = se^2,
    cv = if_else(direct == 0, NA_real_, abs(sqrt(variance) / direct)),
    ci_width = 2 * 1.96 * sqrt(variance)
  )

head(dir_est_bus)

# A tibble: 6 × 5
  domain    direct variance    cv ci_width
  <chr>      <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>
1 W02000001 0.0462  0.00210 0.992    0.180
2 W02000002 0.449   0.0426  0.459    0.809
3 W02000003 0.385   0.0142  0.310    0.468
4 W02000005 0.470   0.0158  0.268    0.493
5 W02000006 0.111   0.0109  0.942    0.409
6 W02000007 0.214   0.0210  0.678    0.569




3.4.2 Evaluation







# A tibble: 6 × 2
  diagnostic           value
  <chr>                <dbl>
1 areas_total        408    
2 areas_no_data        1    
3 areas_n_1            0    
4 median_sample_size  10    
5 median_cv            0.675
6 median_ci_width      0.428





3.5 Final thoughts
In this exercise, all three direct estimates use the same survey-design pattern: create a stratified survey design, estimate by MSOA, and convert the result to the estimand scale where needed. For walk_weekly, SampleTravelWeight is first scaled to a Wales-wide grossing weight, then the weighted total is divided by the known MSOA population and multiplied by 1,000.
This keeps the code consistent across a mean, a share, and a rate-style binary indicator, and it lets the variance estimates account for the survey strata. The population totals are used as known denominators for walk_weekly, but the grossing weight is scaled once for Wales overall rather than separately within each MSOA. That is why the diagnostic checks matter: small-sample area estimates can still be noisy even when the denominator is known.
Precision still needs to be judged from the diagnostics rather than from the estimator alone. In general, the larger the sample sizes, the lower the uncertainty. If many areas have no direct estimate, high CVs, or wide confidence intervals, direct estimation is probably not enough. These direct estimates and sampling variances are then better treated as inputs to model-based SAE.

title: [WIP] Area-Level Estimation (Frequentist)
text: Frequentist area-level SAE is the next step when direct estimates are too noisy or unstable, or when there are estimation areas with weak survey information. The standard starting point is the Fay-Herriot (FH) model, which combines direct estimates with area-level covariates to improve precision.

title: [WIP] Area-Level Estimation (Frequentist)
text:
1 Understanding the Fay-Herriot model
Both frequentist and Bayesian area-level SAE start from the same basic Fay-Herriot idea that the area-level direct estimates are:
\[
\hat{\theta}^{DIR}_d =
x_d^\top\beta + u_d + e_d.
\]
\[
\tilde{\theta}^{FH}_d = \gamma_d \hat{\theta}^{DIR}_d
+ (1-\gamma_d)x_d^\top\beta .
\] The shrinkage weight \(\hat{\gamma}_d = \frac{\hat{\sigma}_u^2}{\hat{\sigma}_u^2 + \psi_d}\), where \(\psi_d\) is the sampling variance of the direct estimate, controls how much the FH estimate “trusts” the direct estimate:

if \(\psi_d\) is small, the direct estimate is precise, so \(\hat{\gamma}_d\) is closer to 1;
if \(\psi_d\) is large, the direct estimate is noisy, so \(\hat{\gamma}_d\) is closer to 0.

In practice, FH leaves precise direct estimates mostly unchanged, while pulling noisy direct estimates toward the covariate-based prediction.
The difference is how the unknown quantities are estimated and how uncertainty is interpreted.

In the frequentist approach, unknown quantities such as \(\beta\), \(\sigma_u^2\), and \(u_d\) are estimated as fixed unknowns. Typical outputs are one point estimate per area (EBLUP), uncertainty measures such as RMSE, and confidence intervals.
In the Bayesian approach, these unknown quantities are instead treated as uncertain and assigned prior distributions. Typical outputs are posterior means, credible intervals, and probability summaries.

This means that, with a Bayesian approach, each area has a distribution of plausible values. This makes it natural to report a central estimate, an uncertainty interval, and probability-style summaries, such as how likely an area is to be above a certain policy threshold.
Therefore, in practice, there is little need to perform Area-Level SAE using both approaches sequentially, especially when there are suitable R packages that have simplified the more complex implementation of the Bayesian approach.






Note



In this frequentist FH workflow, the main outputs are the model-based estimate (EBLUP) and its uncertainty (RMSE).

If EBLUPs stay close to precise direct estimates and RMSE is acceptable, the estimates may be suitable for reporting.
If results are sensitive to covariate choice or diagnostics show poor fit, revisit the covariates and refit the model.

title: [WIP] Area-Level Estimation (Frequentist)
text:
2 Code Implementation






Tip



Use sae::eblupFH() to fit a frequentist Fay-Herriot model and sae::mseFH() to estimate uncertainty.
sae does not handle out-of-sample areas (i.e., estimation areas without respondent records) automatically. These areas require separate model-based predictions from the fitted covariate relationship.



2.1 Worked Example
Lorem Ipsum - share real example National Survey of Wales

title: SAE Toolkit Lite
text: SAE Toolkit Lite is meant for quick adaptation of the materials to another survey, geography, or indicator. It assumes an area-level SAE workflow and a Bayesian modelling approach.

title: SAE Toolkit Lite
text:
1 Minimum inputs and recommended schema
A SAE workflow usually needs:

a survey indicator file, with one row per sampled unit used in the estimate
a domain-size file, with one row per estimation area
a covariate file, when fitting an area-level model
boundary geometry to map results or derive spatial structure, if relevant
(optional) population unit file when fitting unit-level SAE


1.1 Survey indicator file:
A survey indicator file links each sampled unit to the chosen outcome, estimation area, and survey weight. Survey documentation should be kept alongside the file so that routing, missingness, design variables, and weight choice can be checked.



Column
Meaning




unit_id
Survey respondent, household, or sampled unit ID.


domain_id
Estimation area code.


indicator
Numeric outcome, or binary 0/1.


weight
Survey weight selected for the estimand.


stratum
(optional) survey stratum.





1.2 Domain-size file
If available, a domain-size file gives the denominator for each estimation area. The denominator must match the estimand’s target population rather than the full resident population by default.



Column
Meaning




domain_id
Estimation area code.


domain_size
Denominator matching the target population.





1.3 Geometry file
Boundary geometry is useful for visualising estimates and for creating adjacency matrix for spatial models.







Column
Meaning




domain_id
Estimation area code, matching the domain-size file.


domain_name
Human-readable area name.


geometry
Polygon or multipolygon geometry for the estimation area.





1.4 Covariate file for area-level estimation
Covariates are area-level predictors available for every estimation area. They should be measured on the same geography as the direct estimates, or aggregated to that geography before modelling.







Column
Meaning




domain_id
Estimation area code.


covariate_*
Area-level predictor available for every estimation area.





1.5 Files for unit-level estimation
Unit-level SAE needs two harmonised files:

The survey_unit_data file besides indicator also needs covariates used to fit the model








Column
Meaning




unit_id
Survey respondent, household, or sampled unit ID.


domain_id
Estimation area code.


indicator
Outcome to model.


weight
Survey weight selected for the estimand.


stratum
(optional) survey stratum.


covariate_*
Individual or household covariate.




The population_unit_data file provides the prediction population (real or synthetic microdata), with one row per population unit, household, or population cell. The covariates must match those in survey_unit_data.








Column
Meaning




unit_id
Population person, household, or synthetic unit ID.


domain_id
Estimation area code used to aggregate predictions.


covariate_*
Matching individual or household covariate also used in survey_unit_data.


pop_weight
Population count represented by the row (optional, in case population cells are used)





1.6 Recommended output schema
For diagnostics, each estimation script should end with an area-level results table using the same column names. This makes it easier to combine direct, area-level, spatial, and unit-level outputs.







Column
Meaning




domain_id
Estimation area code.


method
Estimation method label.


estimate
Point estimate for the estimation area.


se
Standard error or posterior standard deviation, where available.


lower
Lower confidence or credible interval bound.


upper
Upper confidence or credible interval bound.

title: SAE Toolkit Lite
text:
2 Downloadable scripts
Reusable scripts can be adapted once the input files follow the schema above.



Script
Purpose




direct_estimates.R
Calculate direct estimates by estimation area, sampling variances, and confidence intervals.


area_level_sae.R
Fit Bayesian area-level model with covariates and spatial structure derived from boundary geometry, if relevant.


unit_level_sae.R
Fit Bayesian unit-level model using survey microdata and population or synthetic-population covariates.


diagnostics.R
Create comparison tables and plots for checking estimates, uncertainty, and model outputs.
